slides.mascot_explanation: Now comes the fun part - you get to be the table-setting expert! Take your time and make everything look just right. I'm so proud of how careful and thoughtful you're being!

items: Can you tell me where the fork goes?
What do we put down first before anything else?
Where does the cup belong on our table setting?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're becoming such a table-setting pro! Let's see how much you remember about where everything goes. I bet you know all the special places!

slides.text: You've learned to create a beautiful, complete place setting! Your family will be so impressed when you help set the table for meals.
slides.mastery_check: Child can independently place all items in correct positions for a complete table setting.
slides.mascot_explanation: Wow, what a wonderful table-setter you've become! Your family is going to feel so special when they see the beautiful table you've made for them. You should feel very proud of this important grown-up skill!

title: Building CVC Words with Moveable Alphabet: Short A Family
description: Children will use the Moveable Alphabet to construct simple three-letter words from the short 'a' word family. This hands-on approach to word building strengthens phonetic awareness and prepares children for independent reading and writing.
instructions: Set up the Moveable Alphabet letters in alphabetical order on a mat or tray
Start with the word 'cat' - have your child find and place the letters c-a-t
Sound out each letter as they place it, then blend the sounds together
Guide them to build 'bat' by replacing the 'c' with 'b'
Continue with 'mat', 'rat', 'hat', and 'sat' using the same method
Encourage your child to sound out each word they create
Let them choose additional short 'a' words to build independently
End by reading all the words they've built together
parent_notes: The Moveable Alphabet allows children to compose words before they can write them, separating the mental work of spelling from the physical challenge of writing. This follows the Montessori principle of isolating difficulty. Go slowly and let your child think through each sound. If they struggle, help by emphasizing the individual sounds in each word. This concrete manipulation of letters builds a strong foundation for abstract reading and writing skills.

slides.heading: Building CVC Words with Moveable Alphabet: Short A Family
slides.subheading: Creating words with our hands and sounds
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, word builders! I'm Betsy Bear and I can't wait to help you make real words with these special letters! We're going to be like word architects today, building words piece by piece.

slides.setup_instructions: Arrange the Moveable Alphabet letters in alphabetical order on the mat, with frequently used letters easily accessible.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these wonderful letters waiting to become words! Each letter has its own special sound, and when we put them together, magic happens - they become words we can read!

slides.text: Find the letters c-a-t and place them in order while saying each sound aloud.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's start with one of my favorite animals - a cat! Listen carefully to each sound: /c/ /a/ /t/. Can you hear how each letter makes its own special sound?

slides.text: Replace the 'c' with 'b' to make 'bat', sounding out the new word as you build it.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we're going to do something really cool - we can change just one letter and make a completely new word! It's like a word magic trick that you get to do!

slides.prompt: Build the words 'mat', 'rat', 'hat', and 'sat' by changing the first letter each time. Sound out each word as you complete it.
slides.mascot_explanation: You're becoming such a fantastic word builder! See how all these words have the same /a/ sound in the middle? That makes them part of the same word family - they're like word cousins!

items: What sound does the letter 'a' make in these words?
Can you build the word 'pat' with your letters?
What happens when we change the first letter in a word?
slides.mascot_explanation: You know so much about building words now! Let's see what you remember about our short 'a' word family. I'm excited to hear your smart thinking!

slides.text: You've successfully built multiple words from the short 'a' family using the Moveable Alphabet. You're well on your way to becoming an independent reader and writer!
slides.mastery_check: Child can independently build at least 4 three-letter words with short 'a' sound using Moveable Alphabet.
slides.mascot_explanation: What an amazing word architect you are! You built so many real words today with your own hands and your smart brain. Soon you'll be reading and writing words everywhere you go!

title: Hundred Board: Finding Missing Numbers in the 70s Row
description: Children will work with the Hundred Board to identify and place missing numbers specifically in the 70s row. This activity strengthens number sequence understanding and pattern recognition while building familiarity with two-digit numbers in the seventies.
instructions: Set up the Hundred Board with all numbers in place, then remove 4-5 numbers from the 70s row only
Place the removed number tiles in a small bowl beside the board
Point to the first empty space and ask your child what number belongs there
Guide them to look at the numbers before and after the empty space for clues
Have them select the correct number tile and place it in the empty space
Continue with each missing number, encouraging them to count or use patterns
Once complete, have your child read the entire 70s row aloud: 70, 71, 72, etc.
Repeat the activity by removing different numbers from the 70s row
parent_notes: The Hundred Board is a powerful Montessori material that helps children see numerical patterns and relationships. Focus only on the 70s to avoid overwhelming your child while building confidence with this specific decade. Encourage them to use the surrounding numbers as clues rather than counting from 1 each time. This develops mathematical reasoning and pattern recognition. The concrete manipulation of number tiles supports abstract number concepts and prepares for later addition and subtraction work.

slides.heading: Hundred Board: Finding Missing Numbers in the 70s Row
slides.subheading: Becoming number detectives in the seventies
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there, number detective! I'm Betsy Bear and I need your help solving a number mystery! Some numbers have gone missing from our Hundred Board, and I know you can help me find them!

slides.setup_instructions: Place the Hundred Board with all numbers, then remove 4-5 tiles from the 70s row and place them in the bowl.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our special Hundred Board! It's like a number neighborhood where every number has its perfect home. But oh my - some numbers from the 70s street have wandered away!

slides.text: Look at the first empty space in the 70s row and examine the numbers on either side for clues.
slides.mascot_explanation: When we're number detectives, we look for clues all around the empty space! The numbers on both sides are like helpful neighbors telling us who lives in between them.

slides.text: Select the correct number tile from the bowl and place it carefully in the empty space.
slides.mascot_explanation: You found it! I love watching you think like a real mathematician, using the pattern to figure out exactly which number belongs in that special spot.

slides.prompt: Find and place all the missing numbers in the 70s row, using number neighbors and counting patterns as your clues.
slides.mascot_explanation: You're such a fantastic number detective! Each time you find a missing number, you're using your brain to see the patterns that make math work. Keep going - you've got this!

items: If 76 and 78 are in their places, what number is missing between them?
Can you count from 70 to 79 without looking at the board?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let me see how much you know about the 70s numbers now! You've become such an expert with these numbers. I'm so impressed with your number detective skills!

slides.text: You successfully found all the missing numbers in the 70s row using patterns and number relationships. You're developing strong number sense!
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify and correctly place missing numbers in the 70s row using number patterns and context clues.
slides.mascot_explanation: What an amazing number detective you are! You solved the mystery of the missing 70s numbers using your smart thinking and pattern skills. The Hundred Board is lucky to have such a careful mathematician helping out!

title: Montessori Bell Grading: Arranging 8 Bells by Pitch from Low to High
description: Children will explore the musical concept of pitch by arranging eight Montessori bells in order from lowest to highest sound. This sensorial music activity develops auditory discrimination and introduces basic musical concepts through hands-on exploration.
instructions: Set up the 8 Montessori bells in random order on a table or mat
Demonstrate how to strike a bell gently with the mallet to create a clear sound
Strike two bells with very different pitches and ask your child which sounds higher
Start with the lowest-sounding bell and place it on the left side of your workspace
Have your child find the next lowest bell by comparing sounds systematically
Continue building the sequence from low to high, left to right
Once all bells are arranged, play the scale by striking each bell in order
Encourage your child to sing along with the ascending pitches
parent_notes: The Montessori bells refine auditory discrimination and introduce musical concepts through sensorial exploration. This follows the Montessori principle of learning through the senses before abstract concepts. Allow your child to work slowly, striking bells multiple times to make comparisons. Some children may find this challenging - that's normal. The goal is developing careful listening skills, not perfect pitch. This activity prepares for later music theory and develops concentration through auditory focus.

slides.heading: Montessori Bell Grading: Arranging 8 Bells by Pitch from Low to High
slides.subheading: Creating beautiful music through careful listening
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, little musician! I'm Betsy Bear and I'm so excited to explore these magical bells with you! Each bell has its own special voice - some high, some low, and we get to put them in order!

slides.setup_instructions: Arrange the 8 bells randomly on the work mat with the mallet easily accessible.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these beautiful bells waiting to make music! Each one is special and makes its own sound. We need to listen very carefully to hear the differences - it's like a sound treasure hunt!

slides.text: Strike each bell gently with the mallet and listen carefully to the sound it makes.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's wake up each bell with a gentle tap! Listen to how each bell sings its own special song. Some voices are low like a papa bear, and some are high like a baby bear!

slides.text: Find the lowest-sounding bell by comparing different bells and place it on the far left.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we're going to be sound detectives! We need to find the bell with the deepest, lowest voice - that's like the papa bear bell. It gets to be first in our sound family!

slides.prompt: Continue finding and arranging the bells from lowest to highest pitch, comparing sounds carefully before placing each bell.
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such careful listening! Take your time and trust your ears - they're getting smarter about music with every bell you place. It's amazing how you can hear the differences!

items: Can you point to the bell that makes the highest sound?
What happens to the sound as we go from left to right?
Can you strike the bells in order to make a musical scale?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's see what your smart ears have learned about these musical bells! You've become such a good listener, and I can't wait to hear what you've discovered about high and low sounds!

slides.text: You've successfully arranged the bells from low to high pitch, creating your own musical scale! Your ears are becoming more sensitive to musical sounds.
slides.mastery_check: Child can arrange 6-8 bells in correct pitch order through careful auditory comparison.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful musician you're becoming! You used your careful listening to put all the bell voices in just the right order. Now we have a beautiful sound family all lined up and ready to make music together!

title: Read Aloud: Lilly's Purple Plastic Purse - Chapter Discussion and Character Feelings
description: Children will listen to Kevin Henkes' story about Lilly's experience with her beloved purse at school and discuss character emotions and problem-solving. This engaging tale explores themes of excitement, disappointment, and making amends through relatable school experiences.
instructions: Gather your child in a comfortable reading space with the book visible
Show the cover and ask your child to predict what the purple purse might be used for
Read the story with expression, pausing to show illustrations clearly
Stop at key moments to discuss Lilly's feelings (excited, angry, sorry)
Ask your child how they would feel in Lilly's situation with the purse
Discuss why Mr. Slinger took the purse and whether it was fair
Talk about how Lilly made things right with her teacher afterward
Connect the story to your child's own experiences with special belongings at school
materials_needed: Lilly's Purple Plastic Purse by Kevin Henkes
Optional: small notebook for story reactions
parent_notes: This Kevin Henkes story deals with complex emotions that children often experience - excitement about something special, disappointment when rules interfere, and the process of making amends. Use this as an opportunity to discuss emotional regulation and problem-solving. The story shows that making mistakes doesn't make someone bad, and that sincere apologies can repair relationships. Connect to your child's own experiences with beloved objects or school situations to deepen comprehension and emotional learning.

slides.heading: Read Aloud: Lilly's Purple Plastic Purse - Character Feelings
slides.subheading: Exploring big feelings through Lilly's school adventure
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi, story friends! I'm Betsy Bear and I have a wonderful book about a little mouse named Lilly who gets a very special purse! I think you'll understand exactly how she feels about her treasures.

items: Lilly's Purple Plastic Purse by Kevin Henkes
Optional: small notebook for story reactions
slides.setup_instructions: Create a cozy reading area where your child can see the book illustrations clearly and feel comfortable discussing the story.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's make our special reading nest! I love how we get comfy together when we share stories. Look at this beautiful book - I can already tell it's going to be a story about feelings and friendship!

slides.text: Look at the cover together and predict what might happen with Lilly's special purple purse.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at Lilly on the cover! She looks so proud and happy with her purple purse. What do you think makes this purse so special to her? I love making guesses about stories before we read them!

slides.text: Read the story aloud, pausing at emotional moments to discuss how Lilly is feeling and why.
slides.mascot_explanation: As we read, let's pay special attention to Lilly's feelings - sometimes she's happy, sometimes upset, and that's just like real life! It's okay to have big feelings about things that matter to us.

slides.prompt: Discuss Lilly's problem with her teacher and how she solved it. Talk about times when you've had similar feelings about something special.
slides.mascot_explanation: Lilly learns something really important in this story - that we can make mistakes and still make things better! Have you ever felt super excited about something special like Lilly did with her purse?

items: How did Lilly feel when her teacher took her purse?
What did Lilly do to make things right with Mr. Slinger?
Why do you think Lilly's purse was so important to her?
slides.mascot_explanation: You understood so much about Lilly's feelings and choices! It's wonderful how you can think about why characters do what they do. That shows you're a really thoughtful listener!

slides.text: You explored how Lilly handled big feelings about her special purse and learned from her mistakes. Stories like this help us understand our own emotions and relationships.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify character emotions and connect story events to personal experiences.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a beautiful story about feelings, friendship, and making things right! Lilly shows us that it's normal to make mistakes, and the important thing is learning how to fix them with kindness. You're such a wise story thinker!
